Should the frame eventually fail, I'll build a stronger one. Ditto for the crank handle. It's the drum and lid that really matter in this unit, and they seem to be all but bulletproof!

Great uses for this thing: T-shirts, socks, and underwear! I'm not sure sure about jeans and sweaters or sweatshirts, though I don't see why it couldn't work...it's just the wringing and drying that'll make those tough to handle. If you do anything that can be a bit messy...water based paints, anything 'bleachy', handle animals that can get messy when you're holding them, or whatever, this can be a handy way to save your clothing. If you've staged the Wonder Wash and a gallon jug of water nearby so it's ready to go, tear off your soiled shirt, apron, etc., toss on a clean one, then immediately dump the affected garment in and go. This is assuming it's a mess that will be 'OK' if an immediate dunking and rinsing in cold water will make the difference and you're a long ways from a washing machine. Work outdoors in the heat and humidity? You can take this, along with a gallon or so of water, to the jobsite with you. Take a clean shirt and a pair of socks, of course. Halfway through a hot and humid day, change your shirt and socks. Fill the Wonder Wash, rinse out your wet, sweaty shirt and socks, drape or hang them somewhere to dry, and they'll be 'almost clean' (not sweaty and smelly, anyway!) and at least mostly dry when it's time to go home.

Another possible use: If you load your own ammunition, especially pistol cartridges, this could be a nice thing to have. Spent cartridges have soot and lead residues from the primer inside of them, and dirt, sand, and grit from the range on the outside (as well as on the inside). You can put them in a vibratory tumbler with ground walnut shells or corncobs, but that is a NOTORIOUS source of lead contamination. Lead cleanup kits utilize a detergent that is high in phosphates, which converts the lead styphnate or lead azide residues into more stable lead phosphate. Such detergents aren't so easy to find anymore, but Cascade dishwasher powder has about 6% phosphates and is good for this purpose as long as you don't get the phosphate free formula! Wash and rinse your brass with Cascade and warm water in the Wonder Wash, drain (rinse the Wonder Wash out thoroughly!), and let the empty cases dry in the sun. Many will be clean enough to reuse right there. Or, if you still want to tumble them (perhaps after decapping the washed cases?), your media will stay cleaner much longer and the plume of dust that will result when you dump the tumbler out will be walnut shell or corn cob dust, with very little to no lead in it.
top_rant.title: Can be worthwhile!

top_rant.text:
As far as induction goes and as far as size, portability, and power goes, this device is adequate and reasonably priced for its power output and ease of use.

The induction stovetop does have problem controlling temperature like some other reviewer have said. This is especially problematic for low settings at 1, 2, and 3. Now, I want to make sure you understand the difference between power setting and temperature setting. There is no such thing as "temperature setting" preset because the temperature is completely dependent of the size of the pot, the amount of ferrous material used, the thickness of the pot, and the amount of water/food in the pot. To set a temperature without knowing these variables is simply impossible. The stove guesses it by assigning a guessed power level to heat the pot. Ok, so know we understand. The only remaining variable to play with is the Power level ranging from 1-10. The stove does not exactly have 10 different power levels -- instead, at power 1, 2, and 3 the stove switches on and off at different intervals. This is like your microwave oven. The 50% setting, for example, is where your microwave turns on and off 50% of the time. The microwave does not have a "half power" microwave emission. So, imaging you turning your gas stove on and off 50% of the time, this is how Induction mimics 50% power setting. Does it still work? Yeah, it does. It's OK. Sure, it's not like turning the gas down to 50% power (true power control), the repeating On/Off/On/Off kinda works similarly enough to mimic a reduction in heat. This is why this stove has difficulty producing a good temperature range at P 1, 2, and 3. There is going to be a big gap between level 5 and level 3-4, and again between 3-4 to level 1. It is not a continuous, smooth adjustment like that of a gas stove. To me, the 10-step adjust really is good for: Super Hot, Hot, Medium, Low. About 5 setting levels that you can PERCEIVE and this perception is made in the pot (the food) itself.

Ok, now we got the technical stuff out of the way. Whew!

Cooking with it: Well, choose your pan/pot size well and beware of heat ring/circle, aka hot spot. A gas stove distributes the gas/heat across the bottom of the pan (the same reason it looses close to 40% of the heat energy into surrounding air). The entire bottom of the pan despite the size of the pan gets rather well-distributed heat. Assuming you have a good heat-distributing layer in your pot/pan (e.g., aluminum, copper, or silicon oil disc), the entire bottom of the pan/pot will be heated evenly. The Induction stove heats via a ring of magnet and the metal directly sits on top of the magnet is heated and nothing else. That's why the stove is cool to touch. And also the reason why Induction is soo energy efficient. But that's the problem here: that ring of metal is heated and nothing else. This means you have a ring of hot metal in the middle of the pan (this is problematic if you are using 12" or greater pan, say 14"). And because the heat energy is SOO efficient, the heat does not distribute (no heat loss) like that of gas, you get that hot spot. I tried 3 different kinds of pans: blue steel, cast iron, and stainless steel. The blue steel was from Matfer, the cast iron was Lodge, and the stainless was All Clad. All these were top-brands and I cooked on all of them on gas. No hot spots. The tri-layer All-Clad has an aluminum core for heat distribution, too. Anyway, the induction stove produced very distinguishable hot spot in all 3 pans. This was not the case on gas.

The hot spot problem is not really an issue on smaller pans: 10", 8" or possibly 6" although I didn't try a 6" pan. Assuming the 6" pan is not too small (smaller than the diameter of the magnet ring) to use.

Heating speed: At 1800 W energy it is equivalent to about 12K BTU stove which is rather impressive. Well, all induction heating is impressive and that's why induction can be the way of the future assuming some of the early-age problems get fixed in subsequent generations of the induction revolution. Each 1kW of induction energy is equivalent to about 7k BTU of gas energy and don't forget there can be as much as 40% energy loss from gas stove. So, this 1.8kW stove produced 12K BTU at full power which is about the same heat as your typical store-bought family-grade gas range--the front burners are usually 12-15k BTU or some 17k BTU (aka Power Boost mode). Imagine a full size family-grade induction stovetop offering 3kW or 3.5kW energy, that's equivalent to nearly 50% hotter than the hottest gas burners currently available in the U.S. So, yes, a pot of water, soup, or sauce heats up very, very fast. I was also able to get the cast iron grill to very high temperature for searing. Or steak cooking.

Responsiveness: Very responsive. Just like gas stove you can turn the heat up and down instantly. Induction does that as well. Immediate response.

Noise? The magnet switching on and off does produce a high-pitched noise like that of a high-powered electronic device. I didn't notice it at first and this noise is not as big of an issue at P8, 9, or 10. (full-on). This noise became noticeable at P5. This is funny--my cats totally noticed. When I cook on gas, they can't wait but hang out next to me waiting for a bite of that chicken or fish. When I tried and cooked with this Induction stove, cats are gone! Seriously! They all sat on the other side of the kitchen and perched. At first I didn't realize this then I realized it was the stove's noise. Now hear me--I am not saying the noise is loud. In fact, it is rather quiet, yes.. but animals hear differently than humans do, we all know that, right? The cats were probably hearing some high-pitched electronic whine that didn't sit well with them so they took off. I don't have a dog so I don't know if a puppy will have issues with the electronic whining noise. So, be aware.

Clean up: Easy to clean up. The surface is smooth and glass, easy to wipe off. Scratch?? You bet. If you like to shake your pan when cooking, you've got some new learning curves. Do not shake and toss your pan too much on the glass surface. YES, OVER TIME, YOU CAN SCRATCH the surface of glass Induction cooktop. The heavier the rougher the pan the worse--e.g. Cast Iron,

NEW INNOVATIVE cooking idea: Put a layer of kitchen towel directly on top of the stove when cooking. What?!?! yep. you heard me right. You can put down 1 layer of towel directly on the cooktop and put your pan on top of the towel. The magnet will heat up the metal (the pan) but not the towel. Of course, after 20-30 minutes the heat of the pan will heat up the towel so careful not to set your kitchen on fire!!! But why would you do this?? Imaging cooking pasta sauce. Or making bacon. The splatters! The oil splatters, the sauce, the ... All that droplets of oil and grease all over your cooking surface, right? Lay down towel, and voila! The splatter all caught on the towel! This is something you cannot do with gas or electric stove! Only induction! Now make sure the towel is not too thick. Too thick of a towel creates a gap between the pan and the stove which the machine will shut off (a safety mechanism). As soon as the pot is taken off the induction surface the magnet stops. Anyway, this is an innovative idea of using induction and keeping the kitchen clean. AGAIN, BE CAREFUL! THE HEAT OF THE PAN CAN STILL TRANSFER TO TOWEL and Do NOT Burn Down Your Kitchen!

For that matter, maybe a thin layer of aluminum foil could work?? I need to try this!

Why the 3-star then?
1. Induction technology and for the price of this stove you get 1.8kW energy, 5 stars.
2. Lack of precision power control (P1-10 really is only 4 or 5 distinctive level of heat), 2 stars.
3. Magnetic ring design causing hot spot: 2 star.
4. Ease of use, simple, intuitive? 4 stars. Maybe 5.
5. Noise issue? Or..

I think a 3-star meaning "It's Okay" is a reasonable score for this product. Certainly there are worse stoves out there. This product is actually America Test Kitchen's highest recommended one. So if you are OK with the problems described above, this stove is actually a great stove.
top_rant.title: Some learning and some issues.  It's both good and bad.

top_rant.text:
I do not even know where to begin! This review is for the largest (15lb) size of washer.

I do an IMMENSE amount of laundry. Both parents are disabled seniors, plus I've got three male cats (one in his extreme geriatric years), and while most of them TRY SO HARD to be helpful and good I still end up with a ton of laundry. For years I had a front-loader by GE, loved it, but it was horrible to maintain; the filter was upside down, underneath, and strictly speaking required a service call to clean out, but I would do it anyway. When it finally gave up the ghost a year or so ago, I bought an expensive GE monolith that hurt like hell to haul into the back of the house, required two doors to come off their hinges, and lasted a whopping six months before mysteriously giving up the ghost with absolutely no indication of what went wrong. But, it had a filter in the FRONT! :)

So I did without a washer for a while because I was heartsick and am honestly not sure I can get the old washer back out again. Laundromats seemed wonderful at first, get all the laundry done in one go, but slowly and sneakily drained the savings until we literally couldn't afford to wash clothes anymore. Luckily that happened right -at- the same time I got a good enough paycheck from work to afford this little gem.

This washer is a WORKHORSE.

I will plainly admit that I very likely do way more laundry in this washer than it was ever intended to handle. On "bad" days I will do 3-8 tubs, although I do keep a nose peeled and let the washer rest between cycles if it smells like it's heating up. Normal days are 2-3 tubs a day, 4-5 days a week. I expect every day that it's going to die, but it's been four months and quite honestly I've done more laundry in it than I did in the larger, expensive machine. It is also spinning MUCH BETTER at the four-month mark than the larger, more expensive machine, which impresses me no end. It handles full queen sheet sets (just) and everything but the largest, flluffiest blankets. Towel sizes vary from household to household but, for our towels, I'll throw in 2-3 with either a sheet or a few pieces of clothing.

I also do horrible, mischievous things to this poor little machine that I've never done before. I stop cycles early, I change settings mid-cycle, I stop one cycle to start another, I set the timer so it'll wash in the middle of the night, I set the washer to run after I'm done running the roaster for dinner, basically playing with all of the settings in ways I never did on a washer before. I've never used a washer to soak things before, but now I do and it's just so simple. I can leave things soaking when I go to bed and tell it to rinse and spin while I'm catching some sleep. I try to use good sense (does spinning out sheets that are going on the line outside REALLY require the last four minutes of spinning?) but the long and short of it is that I pretty much utilize this washer with clothes every way possible.

With regards to setup: it was so easy to pack and wheel it into place next to the kitchen sink! I did in the drain hose relatively early on, but there's a lovely Harvey universal drain hose here on Amazon that replaces it easily, has a wider diameter, and appears more robust in general. Amazon also sells drain pans if you're worried about spills (which I was after the drain hose incident), all you have to do is measure your space and buy to match. Our washer is not hooked to the tap, but rather to a 15-foot garden hose leading to the original washer's cold faucet, which works just fine for us since I only ever wash in cold anyway. It drains into the kitchen sink and doesn't overflow, unless someone carelessly leaves a dish in to cover the drain. (Not that that has EVER happened!!!!) Because the wiring in the house -and- neighborhood is all very old, it is actually plugged into an exceedingly overrated surge suppressor mounted beside it rather than being plugged into the wall. When not in use, the surge suppressor is turned off, so hopefully this saves our electric bill a wee bit, too.

So, it's a bit of a Rube Goldberg setup, but I love the washer so much, love that it makes my life easier and saves me both money and time. Honestly, when I finally run it into the ground, if I can't fix it myself and I can afford it I'll probably just buy the same machine again. It's cheaper than the laundromat for the amount of washing I do, and it's just so nice to use. I've never had a washing machine that encouraged me to -play- before! I also love, without end, that it has -two- filters and they're both easy to access. THANK YOU FOR THAT, PANDA!

In the meantime I'll be saving up for a Panda dryer; line drying in the Winter is driving me CRAZY!

If I had any wishes for the washer, it would be:
- A 20-minute cycle for things actually dirty but really not THAT dirty. (I pretty much just use the 10-minute cycle as an extra "did-I-really-add-that-much-soap" rinse cycle.)
- A spin cycle without draining the tub first, for when I really just want to spin something a little bit more.
- An extra soap cup so that I can split a "pod" easily between the two and then just switch them when I put on the next tub of laundry.

These are the various detergents and additives I've used at different times in the washer. All seem to work quite well, although you do need to be careful with proportions; the washer is much smaller than standard, so save full-sized "pods" for really soiled loads!
- Arm & Hammer Liquid Laundry Detergent Plus OxiClean
- Arm & Hammer Laundry Detergent Plus OxiClean Power Paks
- OxiClean Versatile Stain Remover (Bulk Powder)
- OxiClean 2-in-1 Stain Fighter Power Paks
- Tide Odor Rescue with Febreze
- Tide Brights Plus Whites Rescue
- Downy Ultra Liquid Fabric Softener, Free & Sensitive (Using a Downy Ball.)
- Febreze In-Wash Laundry Odor Eliminator
- Tide PODS Free & Gentle
- ALL Mighty Pacs Laundry Detergent, Free Clear OXI
- Tide Washing Machine Cleaner (This works best if it is the last thing you run before you go to bed at night.)
- Clorox Color-Safe Bleach
- Bounce Bursts In-Wash Scent Booster

My favorite so far is ALL because it gets clothes clean but leaves them smelling pretty much like tap water, which is super-handy for line-drying indoors; I prefer the scented detergents for things that I hang outside where they're going to be well-aired and only carry a small amount of their scent back into the house. As you can tell from the list, pretty much any style of detergent or additive works in this machine, even with just using cold water. What I use depends entirely upon what I am cleaning up and what I've got around the house at the time. If I'm using anything worryingly corrosive (hello, bleach, my nemesis) I'll just make sure to run a tub of something a little more neutral just afterwards. I really love this washer and want to keep it running for as long as I can!

As a PS, this washer also cleans stuffed toys of ALL sizes and spins out queen-sized goosedown pillows better than the fancy washer ever did. I mean, I have to wash one pillow at a time, but I hang that pillow over a bed rail and it's dry enough to use by bedtime, without having a dryer. I can't even begin to state how stress-relieving that capability is. No emergency runs to the laundromat! It also washes pillows with polyester filling and the ones with little polyester pellets. And, it also washes microplush and chenille blankets, which are VERY, VERY, VERY heavy when wet, and spins them out incredibly well. But, it also is really nice for washing delicates like my striped stockings, although you'll want a mesh bag for anything truly delicate. Seriously. I could sell these washers. It gets everything SO CLEAN!

Edit: I just also wanted to add two things.
1) The washer pretty much did run silent when I first got it, but after the INSANE amount of washing I've done it's definitely audible when it runs. The motor for spinning the tub and the pump(s) can both be heard. Keep in mind, it's purely due to my insane washing load that this has happened so quickly, though.
2) The two filters: One is a small basket inside that will catch small particulates such as sand, lint, and undissolved soap. You'll want to check this before every tub, the first few times you run it, just until you get an idea for how often you'll need to change it. I need to change it more often for towels, shoes, and for things that got actually muddy while working outside.
The other one is on the back of the machine right next to the drain hose. It's a little out-of-the-way to get to, but it's the same sort of filter you'll find on modern front-loaders: unscrew the cap, pull out the unit to see if there's anything big caught in it, and screw it back in -- FIRMLY. You want that cap in all of the way because that's right in front of the pump and if you don't get it in securely the washer will just drain water EVERYWHERE. You probably won't need to check this one all that often, though, it's more a semi-annual maintenance thing.

If anyone has any questions, let me know and I will do my best to answer them!
top_rant.title: The Marathon Runner of Washing Machines!

top_rant.text:
As I prepared to purchase a new range, I did a lot of research on the internet. While there were many glowing reports for the cafe dual fuel, there were just as many highly critical reports. What explained such a discrepancy? Bad quality control? Maybe. Inexperienced users? Perhaps. People expecting one thing and getting another? Possible, I suppose. I didn't know. But I noticed the same thing for all brands and all types of ranges. It would seem that there is no range that is universally recognized as the best. Likewise, I noticed that people get pretty worked up about ranges.

How could I cut through all that and find a range that I really like? The first thing I did was talk to 3 different appliance dealers that sell nearly all brands and all of them said that GE does ranges better than it does anything else and furthermore that GE knows ranges better than most. That was helpful. I was really interested in dual fuel. I also liked the looks of the Cafe. But then on the other hand, my sister liked her Bosch dual fuel range that is similar in style.

Eventually I settled on the GE because it seemed more substantial. I did this tentatively; crossing my fingers; hoping that actual problems were rare. I assumed that those who got stuck with a lemon were more motivated to go on the internet and write something then those who had a stove that worked well.

When my new range was delivered, I was very interested in putting it through its paces and seeing if I agree or disagree with those who wrote against it. Also I wanted to see if I made the right choice.

The chief complaints people seem to have against the GE dual fuel Cafe range are as follows:

1. Burners not calibrated well

2. Lower oven does not heat fast enough.

3. Griddle is not hot enough

4. Control knobs get HOT!

5. The mess! Oh the Mess!

6. Water doesn't boil fast enough!

Here's what I have concluded about these complaints after a couple of day fiddling with the The Cafe:

1. Burner calibration: For me and my range, this complaint was not true. I took a sauce pan, about 8" in diamater, filled it with water, put it on the biggest burner, brought it to a boil. When it come to a boil, I was immediately struck by how even the bubbles were in size and spacing. I've used three different stoves over the years and never saw this before. As I lowered the heat, the bubbles merely got smaller...but still evenly spaced. Wow!. Nice, even heat and such a delicately broad range even in rate of boil! I did this on all the burners and had the same experience. This cooktop has great control. Cooked bacon on it this morning and easily found the sweet spot for perfect bacon....barely sizzling.

2. Lower oven does not heat fast enough: Yep there is some truth to this complaint. But it depends on what is meant by "fast enough". It does require 20 minutes to warm up. This is not the main oven, and I never expected it to perform like my main oven. If I'd wanted two main ovens, I'd get dual wall ovens. I'm just happy and somewhat amazed that I get to have an extra little oven in my storage drawer! (and yes... you can still use it for storage!) It'll be handy the 20 or so times a year when I want to cook two different things at different temperatures. For those times, I'll simply plan ahead and give the lower oven time to warm up. I did a pan of corn muffins (20 minute baking time) in this lower oven as I cooked some ribs(70 minute cooking time) in the bigger oven. As I put the ribs in the big oven, I turned on the lower oven to warm up. I put the corn muffins in so that they would be done the same time as the ribs. Both ribs and muffins turned out great. They don't recommend things like pizza or roasts in the lower oven....no way to clean the upper surface. So only breads, cookies and non splattering things go in the lower oven.

3. Griddle not hot enough: I think this complaint is just a case impatience and a related lack of reading. The manual tells exactly how to set it for each food..and how long to warm it up. For pancakes it is setting 4 with a 10 minute warm up. It made perfect pancakes! Better than my stand-alone griddle (which, by the way, takes 7 minutes to warm up and doesn't have the benefit of my exhaust hood). The cafe griddle is heavy... and gives very even heat across the whole surface. The pancakes I've cooked on it turn out identical and even in color. Never cooked such perfect pancakes. In the reviews, I hear people talking about cooking sausage, bacon, and things that splatter. I would never do that on this griddle in my kitchen. Massive amounts of grease spattering everywhere is most uncool. Funny thing though, GE put grease channels in this griddle as if you'd use it to fry greasy/sizzling things, a sales gimmick no doubt. But pancakes, eggs, french toast, rubens; all would do very well on there. It did a marvelous grilled cheese. But only held three of them (larger sandwich size bread). It's kind of nice having a little griddle ready to go. It's smaller size makes it easy to clean.

4. Control knobs get hot. There is some truth to this complaint. While I was cooking the ribs in the oven, I boiled a big pan of water on the front burner just to see how hot the knobs get as both stove top and oven are used at once...which is the situation that generates this complaint. They do indeed get hot. But I would not say it was a burning temp. I could still hold them constantly....but only on the part that was farthest away from the range (the knobs are long and project outward). Tonight I did convection setting...with pizza in there. Barely any heat in the knobs.

5. Mess! This complaint has some validity too. Range tops do get messy. I suspect that these are first time gas stove users who are making this comments. Gas stoves can be quite a bit harder to keep clean than smooth tops or electrics. But the way this one is constructed, I think the cleaning will be a lot easier than any other gas stove I've had. The burners are sealed and skirted so that the seam between burner and the sheetmetal is not an issue. I'll like not having to try to clean that!

6. Water doesn't boil fast enough: Okay, this is one of consumer report's issues too. They seem to mark all gas ranges down because they don't boil water as quickly as an electric. But I contend that there's a lot more to cooking than boiling water fast! A lot of people don't know this, but gas burners take longer to boil water than electric or ceramic. Yep. it's just a fact. you'd expect all that impressive fire to heat faster, but no. Where gas shines is in it's ability to simmer and saute without scorching. Gas is much gentler and more capable of doing foods just right and at a much higher level of flavor. That's why I like it. If all you do is boil water to heat prepackaged meals, I wouldn't recommend a gas stove. But if you like to cook, to saute, simmer, caramelize, reduce, and deglaze you gotta get gas! One other thing that is more critical with gas is the size of the pan. If you put a little pan on a big gas burner, a lot of heat's going to blast up the sides of the pot.

In Conclusion, the GE Cafe Dual fuel is, so far the most impressive range I've ever owned. To my mind the extra money spent was well worth it. If my impression changes I'll amend this later. I did buy an extended warrantee just to be safe. Seems like appliances don't last as long as they used to.

P.S. Here's what ranges I've used in the past just to give you an idea of what I am comparing to so you can understand where I'm coming from: an Older Hotpoint Electric (from the late 70's/alright ), A GE Gas...(mid-level from '95/Good), and Jenn-air gas (2003..slide in/ very disappointing and found perverse joy in seeing it carted away)

----Update at the 1 year mark February 22, 2011----

After nearly a year of use, my opinion of this range has not changed. It is still the best range I've ever owned. I thought I'd add a few comments that come from my experience over time.

1. Pan size. I wouldn't use anything smaller than 6 inches. Have a revere 4 inch which is useless on it. None of the burners handle really small pans well.

2. Convection bake. I make my own granola, this requires spreading it out in a single layer on two jelly roll pans. In every other oven I've used, I've had to rotate the pans two or three times during the baking in order to achieve even cooking. Not so with this oven in convection mode! I put the pans in on two different shelves and and don't turn them at all. The granola comes out perfect. In addition, instead of having to adjust your time, the oven automatically knocks off 25 degrees when you go into convection. Thus, old recipes that are non-convection can be cooked for the same time period. (I remember one reviewer who complained that his oven never reached the temp he set. Perhaps this is why.)

3. Convection roast. (This differs from convection bake in that it doesn't take 25 degrees off... their might be other technical differences that I am not I don't know about.) One of my family's favorite things is when I cut up potatoes, coat with olive oil, sprinkle with rosemary and garlic and bake at 375, it takes an 60 minutes plus a little extra time under the broiler to give them a golden crust in a conventional oven. But in Convection roast, it takes about 40 minutes with no broiler at the end and it develops the golden crust all by itself. I also did a prime rib with convection roast. Awesomeness! What's cool about the convection roast is not only is it faster and more even, but it seems to preserve the moisture in the food better.

4. Regular bake. I bake a loaf of bread about every three days. (Rose Levy's Flax loaf). Turns out great. Casseroles and other hot dish recipes also work out well.

5. Main big burner in front works great for wok cooking.

6. Cleaning the range top. Easy. Still not as easy to clean as an electric however. The Stainless steel does make it easy to keep clean compared to other gas stoves.

7. I've use the self-clean feature twice. Does what is supposed to.

So far everything still works great. I am still satisfied.

top_screaming.text: PART ADVERTISED AS SAMSUNG OEM - BUT SEEMINGLY NOT. THERE WAS NO SAMSUNG PART NUMBER MARKED ON PART, BUT LOOKED LIKE A MATCH. BE SURE TO UNPLUG DRYER FROM ELECTRICITY BEFORE SERVICING. CHECK OUT YOUTUBE VIDEOS FOR THIS REPAIR. SYMPTOM: DRYER RUNNING WITH NO HEAT. CAUSE: BROKEN HEATING  ELEMENT (ONCE THE HEATING ELEMENT HOUSING WAS REMOVED AND DISASSEMBLED, IT WAS OBVIOUS THAT THE WIRE COIL WAS BROKEN). YOU CAN ALSO CHECK BY MEASURING THE COIL RESISTANCE USING AN OHM METER, PROBING ACROSS THE COIL CONNECTOR LEADS BEFORE DISASSEMBLING THE HEATER ELEMENT HOUSING. TAKE A PHOTO OF COIL ASSEMBLY ELECTRICAL CONNECTIONS BEFORE REMOVING. WEAR GLOVES WHEN HANDLING THE HOUSING, IT HAS A LOT OF SHARP EDGES. BE PREPARED TO SPEND SOME TIME CLEANING THE ACCUMULATED LINT OUT OF THE DRYER CHASSIS. USE A SHOP VAC OR EQUIVALENT TO REMOVE LINT. I ALSO REMOVED THE SCREWS HOLDING THE MOUNTING ASSEMBLY FOR THE CONTROL BOARD (TOP FRONT LEFT) AND CLEANED LINT FROM THE CIRCUIT BOARD COMPONENTS. REASSEMBLING THE HEATING ELEMENT TAKES A LITTLE PATIENCE BECAUSE THE HOUSING METAL IS SPRINGY- USE AN AWL TO ALIGN SCREW HOLES THAT HOLD THE CLAMSHELL HOUSING TOGETHER (7 FOR CLAMSHELL AND 1 EACH FOR THE THERMOSTAT AND FUSE COMPONENTS). RE-INSTALLING THE HEATING ASSEMBLY IS A LITTLE TRICKY AND TAKES SOME PATIENCE - THERE IS A TAB ON THE HEATER COIL ASSEMBLY AND SLOT ON THE BRACKET THAT MUST LINE UP AND MATE - MAKE SURE THAT THE TAB IS NOT BENT OVER (NEEDS TO BE PERPENDICULAR TO CLAMSHELL ASSEMBLY FOR PROPER ALIGNMENT WITH THE SUPPORTING BRACKET). I DID NOT REPLACE THE FUSE AND THERMOSTAT SINCE THEY APPARENTLY WERE NOT THE CAUSE OFBTHE FAILURE (I.E., DONT FIX IT IF IT’S NOT BROKEN). IT’S EASY ENOUGH TO SERVICE AGAIN IF REQUIRED. GOOD LUCK.

top_exclaim.text:
[[VIDEOID:234fb70f4acb15c4ea981c67d77b581d]] This recent summer in NY we experienced the most 90+ degree days with high humidity like crazy!!!😫😫😫😫

I like to watch TV in my livingroom with the AC and a cocktail on the weekends...but the constant back and forth to the kitchen during the heat wave was insane!!!! By the time I got to the fridge I was SOAKED IN SWEAT...one day I just snapped and said THATS IT...I NEED A MINI WINE FRIDGE & MINI ICEMAKER IN THE LIVINGROOM!!!!

BEST IDEA EVER!!!! I keep 2 filtered (Brita Water) bottles of water in my mini wine fridge so I don't have to walk to the kitchen each time it needs to be filled....also I noticed really cold water tends to make it run longer...normal time for large ice cubes is 12 minutes with water around 50 degrees (about 115-120 minutes for the entire bucket to be filled)...it takes much longer for ice cubes to be made if the water is colder. The ice stays a VERY LONG TIME inside the chamber/ice bucket once you get the red/ice full light!!! What's even better is when the ice cubes melt...the water is being recycled back into the icemaker!!! So you really don't have to refill it as much if you think about it😊😊😊😊

NOTE: If the ice cubes are coming out WET its because of 1 or 2 REASONS!!!!
#1 The environment is too WARM...and its NOT VENTILATED PROPERLY!!!!
#2 Icemaker has just been turned on using room temperature water or water from the faucet!!! Wait about 48 minutes (that's 4 12min cycles) for the cubes to stop being wet!!!!

Another plus is that it runs pretty quiet in my opinion...I fall asleep in my livingroom with it running...it does wake me up at times when the ice cubes drop in the plastic ice bucket (but it takes forever for that to happen once the tray is full) I have it stored in a closet and keep the door cracked when in use...when it's not I unplug it and drain the water out!!!

Oh!!! Please don't make the same mistake as I did and PLUG IT IN RIGHT AWAY AFTER ITS DELIVERED!!!! I was so EXCITED that I came up with this concept....I couldn't wait to plug it in...then...read the directions🤦🏽‍♀️🤦🏽‍♀️🤦🏽‍♀️🤦🏽‍♀️
You have to WAIT 1 HOUR before PLUGGING IT IN....the refrigerant has to settle!!! I PANICKED AND QUICKLY UNPLUGGED IT & PRAYED I DIDN'T BREAK IT!!! THANK GOODNESS I DIDN'T!!! 🤞🏽🤞🏽🤞🏽🤞🏽

Anyway I'm so glad I thought of this...I can fully sit back and relax in my livingroom aka girl cave and enjoy a cocktail without having to pause the DVR and walk back & forth to the kitchen!!!
top_exclaim.title: Another FINE ADDITION to my Girl Cave!!!!

top_exclaim.text:
This is a Wonderfully Amazing Machine!!! This is NOT a flimsy little machine. It is a heavy duty ice maker!!! It makes so much ice that I have to turn it off at times because my freezer is small and it gets full quickly!!! This machine has an off and on switch, so you can simply turn it off when you don't need it or when you have enough ice. The small ice cube setting makes a very large ice cube!!! It comes with a nice ice scoop that I keep in a gallon freezer bag in my freezer to dump my fresh ice into so that it will freeze hard. For those complaining about how wet the ice is when it comes out, the ice I buy in bags is wet, too!!! ICE IS WET before it freezes!!! Just put your fresh ice in a baggie, allow it to freeze hard, then break it up by slamming it on your counter, and dump it into your ice bag. At least, you won't have a huge lump of ice cubes all frozen together!!!

This machine is heavy, so take it out of the box close to the table or counter where you plan to use it. You'll want to allow it to sit for 2 hours before using it because the Freon (or coolant) has to settle in the machine. The cleaning instructions are confusing, so I just wiped it down good and filled the reservoir. I threw out all the ice that it made out, drained it, and sopped up all the water, then refilled it. It makes ice very quickly, so soon I was putting ice in the freezer. I am so tickled!!! Now I don't have to remember to buy a bag of ice at the store!!! I am in ICE HEAVEN!!! This is a quality machine, so I don't expect any problems any time soon!!! However, if you are worried about it going out on you, buy the 4 year warranty like I did and then you'll have PEACE of MIND!!!

UPDATE!!! In May 2018, after a year and 2 months of great service, this ice maker died! When I purchased my ice maker, I bought a 4 year warranty thru Square Trade. I have called and they have approved my claim and are going to send me a check!!! I went ahead and bought a new one. I hope that my new ice maker lasts as long as this one did!!!
top_exclaim.title: This is a BIG ICE MAKER and I am so happy that I bought it!!!  No more lugging home bags of ice!!!

top_exclaim.text:
OK, so I have owned and used this unit for approximately 4 months. I wanted to use it for a bit so I could give a more full, accurate review.

I purchased this oven to replace a gas wall oven I had in my kitchen. I prefer the dry heat that an electric oven offers over the wet heat of gas, much better crispy skin on my chickens and turkeys and easier browning on my baked goods! Since my previous oven was gas I did not have the higher watt/volt electrical outlet that most electric ovens require. To get the special outlet installed would have been difficult, at best, and pretty expensive since my electric box is on the opposite side of the house! So started my search for an oven that would work on a regular 110v outlet. (Not necessarily an easy task!) I was so excited when I discovered the Empava brand of ovens! They have several different models, with different cooking options, to choose from to fit your style. The oven arrived in the scheduled time period (fabulous!), but unfortunately was damaged (not so fabulous!). There was a sizable dent on the back panel that prevented the convection oven fan from operating. I contacted the manufacturer and they responded very promptly, willing to work with me to rectify the situation! Talk about customer service!!! They sent me a new oven cavity right away and I received it about a week later. I was disappointed to see that the replacement also had a dent on the back, in the exact same area as the first. I was at my house when both ovens were delivered, and from seeing where the dent was/is and how the deliver guy was carrying the oven, instead of using a dolly to bring it in, I'm certain that a knee was what caused the damage!!! Either from him or some other individual during transport. Anyhow, the dent on the new unit did not hinder the fan or the oven from operating at all! (Since it's able to work on a regular outlet, I plugged it in on my kitchen counter and tested it by baking cookies!) I decided to keep it since the back of the oven was going to be hidden inside the wall cabinet anyway! After I removed the oven door (they sent me instructions on how) and all the accessories (less things to break or cause more damage to the unit in transport) I returned the damaged unit. The manufacturer contacted me to see that the new oven had been received and in good working order. Once I told them about the dent on the new oven they offered to refund me the full price if I wanted to return it or if I wanted to keep the oven they would give me a discount on it. Seriously?! WOW! You don't get service like that offered anymore these days!!! I love all the features on the oven, especially one I didn't even know came on it until after I received the oven.... a rotisserie!!!! So I told them I absolutely wanted to keep the oven. It was already a great price to begin with, but with the discount it was an unbelievable value!!! The contractor I hired to remove the gas oven and install this one was impressed with it's ability to plug into a standard outlet (he said he had never seen one like that before) and he really like the slick look of the black mirrored glass, stainless handle and the digital touchscreen operations! Another bonus of this oven, and I found it out the hard way by crisping a batch of cookies a bit too much, is that it cooks SUPER fast!!! I mean like 40% faster! My cookies which normally take 8-10 minutes only take about 6 minutes now! Although I have to calculate the time and watch most everything I cook in this oven (not necessarily a bad thing!) I am able to get dinner, and dessert, on the table much faster!!! I have yet to try the rotisserie, but I can't wait! (Oh, and the oven comes with a drip pan to catch the juices when you use the rotisserie!!!) Needless to say, I am very pleased with my oven, and the customer service I received from this company was by far some of the best I have ever seen!!! I hope you enjoy this oven as much as I do!
top_exclaim.title: This is a neat little oven!

top_exclaim.text:
I bought this little washer over two months of tedious internet research. I needed a washing machine for my small two bedroom apartment and to do my sons' laundry and my own. There are many portable washing machines out there that are larger or smaller with varying functions and prices, however this machine seemed to fit my needs the best and it was the cheapest!. I just needed a small machine that would wash a reasonable amount of clothes with little to no hassle. I had read some of the other feedback about the machines noisyness, leakness, and hasslesome bottom plate, and extremely small basket and here is what I found....

I ordered the machine and had it within 7 days, I found that reasonable as i did not pay for shipping, it was also delivered with no damage to the machine or the box, I had it shipped fedex so i was able to track it daily.

The machine was very easy to put together out of the box, all I required was a phillips head screw driver. The bottom plate was hasslesome to install but I got it on, after that the rest was cake, I had it up and running within 22 minutes total.

I did a quick wash cycle and the machine ran smooth and strong, adding and draining water as nessacary. I didn't find the machine to be any noisier than any other washing appliance, the machine did rattle slightly durring the spin cycle but I have an area rug underneath it so it was neither noisy or disturbing to me. I experienced no leaks durring this cycle.

the clothes came out clean and wrung out, I was actually very impressed.

The basket size is actually larger than it may seem, I was able to fit into the machine about 3/4 of a standard wash!!!!!

All in all for the price and expectation I gave this machine 5 stars because it does what it say it will do, I have not experienced any hassles with it as of yet and actually I am more than pleased with the machine. I now look forward to never going to the laundry mat again or piled up laundry. I can see in the future having to go to the laundry mat once a month or two months for haevy blankets and rugs but all in all this little machine has handled my washes fine, to include sheets, light weight blankets, and jeans!!! Just distribute the loads carefully, remember one light weight blanket may mean one wash!!!

I am very pleased with this machine!!!! If you would like to see some pictures of it hooked up to the sink so you can get some perspective on it check out my face book page...jaime billiel in charlestown nh...............

UPDATE 2015

Hello all as of my last review I may have told you that I moved and gave my machine to my sister whom till this day FIVE YEARS LATER is still using the little machine :)...... Since then I moved to an apt and purchased a full size set, I met a man and we moved in together to a place with no hook ups so geuss what..... I PURCHASED ANOTHER ONE!!!!!!!!!!!!!! AND USED It for TWO MORE YEARS!!!!!!! Then I bought my very OWN HOUSE with a full size pair (still with my man). And I still HAVE that little machine! LOL

I have an elderly woman who wants to buy it but I dont know if she will follow through or not.

The point of my update, two machines, five years, no problems, still going strong. I may just keep the mini machine if the lady doesnt come to get it for back up, or sell it in a yard sale!!!!!!!!

top_five_star_obscene.text:
Got these as a christmas gift (opened early since my dad loves my cooking and wanted to make it easier for me since I cook for him).

The Good: Set it without anything in the pan other than a little oil and it'll get to that temp in 10 seconds or less as far as I can tell (with their pans, of course). Deep frying is simple to do (not sure what everyone else is complaining about, and I'll explain later what I think their problem is). Because of all involved, I can easily clean a counter top that's flat than a stove top after frying something. Can take them anywhere you have power (cook something smelly outside to keep people inside happy and/or piss off that annoying neighbor that doesn't know how to cook anything but her garbage bags, from the smell of things, is a plus). If you don't have a stove top, this is a must, and perfect for anyone in college. Easy to clean everything. Practically rinse it with scalding hot water right after you empty the contents and wipe dry with a clean towel and you're done (if you wash pans after every use, in stead of that, they're going to turn to crap no matter how good they are)

THE BAD: Nothing is perfect, had eggs stick to them twice on me, but after minor work with a plastic spatula they came off without any casualties, but that can happen on any non-stick since I had one before. The temperature settings are never going to be that accurate when liquids/volume cooking is involved, as I consider calling it. Pans can slide easily off of the surface if you lightly bump them (must be the technology, almost felt air hockey-ish when some olid was under the pan I didn't notice, while moving some food around. The heavier the pan, the flimsier the handle seems to feel on the 16 inch we upgraded to. It doesn't feel like it'll come apart as much as it may bend if you hold it far back on the handle every time, but that's the largest frying pan I've owned, so who knows, could be me. Last but not least, you will be confused on prep and cook time, because this will throw a major curve ball at you, at first. I was skeptical and burnt some oil a couple times on heat up time lol.

in Closing: People complain about the frying. I deep fry in the saute pan or another I have like it. So far, I got a great crispy (yet overcooked, due to my first deep frying attempt ever) chicken, since I bread with egg, flower x2. Still tasted great. Second time came out softer when I worked less on the flower and cooking time. I also tried 350, which gave me a finished softer exterior, great for wings and pizza topping. That's a 25 degree difference. PEOPLE, wake up. There's no way you're going to deep fry in anything in a timely manner unless it's an actual deep fryer. Even then, they're not consistent (chalk up all those days you order wings and wonder why they aren't like last time... yeah. Those are industrial grade fryers too),
I feel the problem people are having with these, like any other cook set, is they expect it to cook for them. Being a chef and a cook (as immature as I am in that field) is like being an artist, and no artist wants their canvas to paint for them. Never leave what you're cooking, even if it's a pizza. I stand there while deep frying and take out piece by piece. If you want convenience... order out lol.

This rocks at steak/searing too, but I recommend using a black iron pan because it gives a different texture and flavor. Ours works on it, but I heard it's not good to use, so I'm not sure if they have one themselves or not. I haven't had to deal with customer service, been happy these past three weeks though, cooking too much to lose the weight I need lol
top_five_star_obscene.title: The nonest truth about these, even though I love them.

When I came across this I immediately thought “how have I never thought of this?” I used to lug around my French press on travel (because I am a coffee snob and crap like Starbucks ain’t gonna cut it). This is such a light and portable way to have great coffee. It is like a cross between pour over and French press. It is also so easy to clean that I’ve found myself using it more often than any of my other brewing methods even at home. Hate putting wet coffee grounds in the trash because it makes the trash rot quicker? Leave the grounds sitting in this thing until they air dry and then dump them in the trash. The flavor is great because it doesn’t capture the oils and leave filter flavor like a paper filter does and although it leaves some sediment in the bottom (which I actually prefer) it captures more than any French press I’ve owned. If you’re getting too much sediment, adjust your grind size. Seriously I didn’t know I could love a tiny piece of plastic this much.

My one concern is that it is made of plastic and I am afraid it could be leaching some as you pour hot water into it. However I’ve had this thing for probably a year or so and it’s seen daily use and hasn’t shown any signs of wear and I never taste any flavors other than coffee so if it is leaching plastic, it’s probably not any more plastic than you’re getting straight from the tap.
top_five_star_obscene.title: Simple, yet effective

top_five_star_obscene.text:
Wonderful range hood at a great price. Friends are envious.

The instructions are not in proper English and are a bit confusing (why is it so hard for manufacturers to get this right in their manuals?). But! do these four things to make installation easy:

1. Throw in the trash the screws and crappy anchors that come with the unit and buy #10 screws with a hex head that you can use a socket on with your drill. So much easier to install this way and you won't risk stripping out the head of your screw in these awkward positions.
2. Temporarily mount a 2x4 on the wall about a half inch below the lower edge of the hood. This gives you the ability to place the hood up there and mark the locations of where the screws go in. I was able to install it myself without a helper (but a helper is nice to have). Pre-drill your screw holes. When you're ready to mount the hood, install two screws where the keyhole slots are in the hood, but leave them sticking out about 3/8". This will allow you to lift the hood up onto the 2x4 and then wiggle the hood over the screw heads, and slide it back into the narrow end of the keyhole slot. Tighten those two screws. Then install the other other two screws that have a plain hole in the hood top.
3. Before you install the hood you need to drill a 1.5 inch hole in the cabinet where the power cord will run. Put a dot of lipstick on the cord right where it would be the center of the hole. Put the hood up on the 2x4 and then let the dot of lipstick touch the bottom of the cabinet. That's the center of where you drill your 1.5 inch hole.
4. If the rear vertical panel of the hood will be screwed into drywall and not a stud, get drywall anchors that have the coarse screw anchors (the ones that you screw into the drywall, they have a lot of holding power). Get the larger ones rated for 75 pounds or so.

I used four screws on the top, and only two on the rear vertical panel of the hood (there's four holes, but only two were easily accessible for screwing).

One more thing (and this was poorly explained in the instructions). The bottom of the cabinet that you attach the hood to needs to be a solid flat surface. My cabinet had a lip on it (most will, it's just part of cabinetry-making). So you'll need to screw some wood in there so the hood rests agains a solid, even, and flat surface). It doesn't matter what kind of wood because it won't show. Just make sure it is at the same level as the cabinet lip.

Good luck!

PS: To the manufacturer: You should include a lighweight cardboard template with this unit. It would make installation so much quicker and easier. You'd get a lot of love and compliments for doing that. And let me rewrite your owner's manual for you!
top_five_star_obscene.title: Great fan!  But here's four important tricks to installing

top_five_star_obscene.text:
When I bought my Keurig at Costco about 5 years ago it came with this refillable big gray cup. I had to remove the black piece with the pin to make it work. It was 3 pieces and a little annoying to clean. After using that for a couple months I went to strictly regular K-cups. I had no idea a product like this was even on the market. After using it a bit here’s what I think:

• No hassle: This is almost as easy as throwing a regular K-cup in. I just fill it up to the top of the mesh. Align it where the poking pin fits in the groove in the bottom of the purple cup, which is very easy. Put the handle down and I’m ready to go. I don’t have to take the black piece out of the Keurig. I didn't find a single coffee ground in my cup of coffee.
• Cleaning: To clean I shake out the grinds in the garbage and put the cup on a peg in the top of the dishwasher.
• Construction: I think these are made pretty well, but they are a thin plastic that I’m guessing will withstand about 3-6 months of use. I will update when they start breaking. Given that they are about $6 a piece the quality is on par for that price. The lid takes a little pressure to get it all the way closed.
• Value: I buy Costco K cups and this product is about what I pay for 50 cups (100 of them is about $33). I know out of these three cups I’ll get a hundreds of cups of coffee. I only buy Costco K-cups because their price is so competitive, but I’m glad I have these little reusable ones now and can go buy some other brands of coffee.
• Storage: These fit in my K-cup carousel, so they are very easy to store. I like that I can throw them in the dishwasher and put them in the holder right when I take them out.
• Green factor: I am not a crazy environmentalist. But I really do think about my impact on the environment, enough that I bought an electric car a year ago. I’m guessing if I added up every K-cup I’ve ever used I would have 4 large garbage cans full. Two cups a day doesn’t look like much in my garbage can, but multiply that by everyone who uses a Keurig and that’s a lot of trash in garbage dumps.

I was really debating between 4 and 5 stars because they are a little flimsy. I am settling on 5 stars because I really do “love” them more than “like” them. They are going to save me money, give me some opportunity to buy different coffee and are so much better for the environment. I was received these at a discount price for testing and review purposes. That has no bearing on my rating and review, this is my honest opinion of the product.
top_five_star_obscene.title: These are very easy to use and great for the environment. Much better than the reusable one I got years ago.

top_five_star_obscene.text:
We chose this and its dryer mate because the NY Times reviewed it as best for the year model for cleaning cloths, gentleness on cloths, and expected durability and longevity of the product line. This is a DAY1 review.

First the Bad:

1. the manuals are terrible. There is so much missing about how to use the controls it is pitiful. Consider for example that there is NOTHING in the dryer manual to tell you how to use all the potential button settings. None of the buttons worked, until I figured out hours later that you must use the MANUAL selection on the dial before you can change stuff.. If you go to the LG web site, there are other documents you can read but I mean really. As a Software Engineer, if my QA and DOCUMENTATION people wrote manuals this bad about what I build, I would fire them.

2. availability is spotty on both the appliances and their accessories. We had trouble finding the matching dryer, and could not get the pedestal add ones because none were available. Chalk this up to COVID I guess but one outfit said we would need to wait five weeks to the matching dryer. So do some research and shop around to see who has what in stock. If your current washer/dryer are on their last leg, maybe don't wait for them to fail before you but new ones? Just a thought.

3. the washer is louder that both the wife and I expected. OK in fairness, there was never any claim made about how loud or quiet these machines were supposed to be. And the door is still off the laundry room and the room is empty... so there is nothing to muffle the sound. I expect this to reduce significantly once the crap is put back into the laundry room and the door hung. So maybe I am over stating this negative.

4. 4.5 cubic feet ain't as big as it sounds. Compared to our old top loader, it seems the size of loads we are putting in the washer is smaller. It is hard to judge without a side-by-side comparison, and maybe we over stuffed to old guy whish is why it had some problems, and I could in fact stuff more into the new machine if I wanted. But it does seem like the load size smaller.

The GOOD:

1. new looking cloths, fast. I kid you not when I say the stuff that comes out of washer and dryer looks brand new. I am a work-from-home IT guy so I am mostly a sweats and t-shirt man. All my cloths so far look brand new and soft out of the dryer. And the "seemingly" smaller load sizes get done in 30 minutes in the washer unless I pick one of the longer running cycles. We did upholstery covers, blankets and bedding, pillows, towels, slippers, and lots of cloths (yeah they delivered the machines @9:30am and I did 10 loads today) and ALL of it looked NEW after being cleaned by this pair of machines. The dryer takes about 54 minutes to dry whatever you put in it. I used the manual setting for a lot of it which allowed me to reduce heat and dry time to 40 minutes.

2. no-brainer operation. If you are not into all the options worry not, you don't really need them. There are so many presets on the dial, you almost can't fail to find a setting that works for whatever you are cleaning. You just turn the machine on, put a detergent plug into the drum, load, dial up your clothing match, and press the run button. The washer appears to have some AI that checks the washer load to see if any timing adjustment is needed off the baseline you dialed in, but maybe that is my imagination. Point is anyone can use these with great precision without any effort. Kind of makes up for the crappy manuals. Only thing I add is "extra rinse" at the end of the wash cycle.

3. has less expensive versions in the model line if you do not want to spend the money on these. I am guessing of course but I would think "lower end" models in the same product line are really just fewer features but same washing and drying ability and reliability of product. So if you need to save money on your set look at those instead.

Tomorrow is the Wife's turn. If she has any major comments I will add them to this review later. Kevin
top_five_star_obscene.title: ONLY a First Day review... all is good though.
